ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for Eggs Royale; mapped to parent class 'egg dish' (FOODON_00002473) as the closest defensible anchor. Eggs Royale is a variant of Eggs Benedict with smoked salmon substituted for Canadian bacon.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW (egg-based dish) used as the closest defensible category anchor. No specific FoodEx2 entry exists for Eggs Royale.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to Eggs Royale as a prepared dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Eggs Royale is approximately 350 g, comprising two sous vide or poached eggs on a garlic baguette with smoked salmon, honey mustard sauce, a side of potato wedges, and salad.

nutrient_source.title: Eggs Royale — Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient record (G00010457)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, carbohydrate) taken directly from HPB Foodo record G00010457. Fat value of 0.0 g/100g is as reported by HPB; this is flagged as nutritionally implausible for a dish containing smoked salmon, eggs, and butter-based sauce — see notes. Secondary nutrients (saturated fat, dietary fibre, sodium, sugar) estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy for Eggs Benedict with smoked salmon (USDA SR Legacy #21340 and related entries) due to absence of detailed micronutrient breakdown in the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Eggs Royale. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on analogous mixed egg-and-bread dish with protein-dominant macros. The high protein content (10.7 g/100g) from eggs and smoked salmon significantly attenuates postprandial glucose response. Baguette base contributes refined carbohydrate (GI ~70 for white baguette alone), but the overall dish GI is moderated by protein and fat co-ingestion. Analogous reference: mixed egg-bread dish, GI ~50–55, University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving = (52 × 2.36 × 3.5) / 100 = 4.3 g (low).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from smoked salmon and sauce
hypertension.guidance: Sodium is estimated at approximately 420 mg/100g (up to 1470 mg per 350 g serving) driven by smoked salmon and honey mustard sauce; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request fresh salmon instead of smoked, and reduce sauce to keep sodium intake within the 600 mg/meal target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate with high protein moderating glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Carbohydrate is low at 2.36 g/100g and the high protein content (10.7 g/100g) from eggs and smoked salmon slows gastric emptying and attenuates postprandial blood glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can include this dish in moderate portions while monitoring the refined carbohydrate contribution from the baguette base.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Omega-3 benefit from smoked salmon offset by sodium and saturated fat from sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Smoked salmon provides cardioprotective EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids, but the high sodium content and saturated fat from butter-based sauce represent cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request reduced sauce, choose fresh salmon over smoked, and limit portion to one serving per occasion.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids from smoked salmon
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Smoked salmon provides EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids that reduce joint inflammation; eggs supply vitamin D and protein for bone protection, making this dish broadly suitable for rheumatoid arthritis when sauce portion is limited to reduce saturated fat and sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Eggs Royale is a Western brunch and lunch dish consisting of sous vide or poached eggs placed on slices of smoked salmon atop a toasted garlic baguette, served with a homemade honey mustard sauce, potato wedges, and a side salad. It is a variant of the classic Eggs Benedict, with smoked salmon substituted for Canadian bacon or ham, giving the dish a distinctly savoury, slightly smoky flavour profile. The eggs are typically cooked sous vide at 63–65°C to achieve a silky, custard-like yolk, while the baguette provides a crisp, garlicky base. The honey mustard sauce adds a sweet-tangy note in place of the traditional hollandaise. Nutritionally, the dish is protein-rich at approximately 10.7 g/100g, with low carbohydrate at 2.36 g/100g, making it a relatively low-glycaemic meal. The smoked salmon contributes omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) with anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective properties. Sodium content is elevated due to the cured salmon and sauce, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for those with fish, egg, gluten, or mustard allergies. In Singapore, Eggs Royale appears on Western café and brunch menus and is popular as a weekend brunch or light lunch option.
common_user_queries: How is Eggs Royale different from Eggs Benedict?
Is Eggs Royale suitable for diabetics?
How much sodium is in Eggs Royale?
Is Eggs Royale high in protein?
Can I eat Eggs Royale if I have high blood pressure?
What are the calories in Eggs Royale?
What sauce comes with Eggs Royale?

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, carbohydrate) are sourced directly from HPB Foodo record G00010457 with high confidence. However, the reported fat value of 0.0 g/100g is nutritionally implausible for a dish containing smoked salmon, eggs, and a butter-based or oil-based sauce; this value is preserved as reported but flagged. Secondary nutrients (saturated fat, dietary fibre, sodium, sugar, potassium) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entries, reducing overall confidence to medium. GI is an analogous estimate.
notes: fat_g is reported as 0.0 g/100g in the source HPB Foodo record G00010457. This value is nutritionally implausible for Eggs Royale, which contains smoked salmon (typically 4–13 g fat/100g), eggs (approximately 10 g fat/100g), and a sauce containing oil or butter. The value is preserved as reported from the approved source but is flagged for review and re-measurement. A realistic fat estimate for this dish would be approximately 4–8 g/100g. fat_g is reported as confirmed zero per HPB source G00010457; however, this is flagged as likely a data entry error. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this specific Singapore HPB Eggs Royale preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. Sodium (420 mg/100g), potassium (180 mg/100g), sugar (0.5 g/100g), dietary fibre (0.4 g/100g), and saturated fat (0.5 g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy for a mixed egg-and-smoked-salmon bread dish. This is a Western dish served in Singapore; multilingual local-language names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable as no established local-language translations exist for this Western brunch dish — English is the sole operative name in the Singapore context.
